title: Escovitch fish
subtitle: Jamaican fried fish with pickled vegetables
excerpt: Mackerel fillets seasoned, fried until crisp, then covered while still hot with a sharp, allspice-scented pickle of onion, carrot, scotch bonnet, and vinegar. The pickle is the centrepiece — the fried fish is the vehicle. Serve at room temperature with festival dumplings or hard-dough bread. The dish improves as it sits.
sourceNotes: Escovitch (also spelled escoveitch) is derived from the Spanish escabeche technique of preserving fried fish in an acidulated vegetable pickle, brought to Jamaica through Spanish colonial presence. The Jamaican version uses allspice and scotch bonnet rather than the Mediterranean herbs of the original, giving the dish a distinctly Caribbean character. It appears in Jamaican cooking literature from the 18th century onward.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Better made 2 to 3 hours ahead and served at room temperature. The pickle penetrates the fish as it cools.

content.text: The pickle must go on the fish while it is still hot from the pan. The residual heat softens the vegetables very slightly and drives the vinegar into the flesh. If you wait until the fish has cooled, the vegetables sit on top rather than seasoning the fish.

Mackerel is an excellent choice for escovitch — its firm oily flesh holds up to the vinegar without becoming mushy. Red snapper or any firm white fish (whole or filleted) can be used. The flour coating gives the fish its crisp surface that holds the pickle.

Pat the {{mackerel-fillets}} dry and season with {{sea-salt-fine}} and {{black-pepper}}. Dust lightly in {{plain-flour}} and shake off the excess. Heat the {{vegetable-oil}} in a large frying pan over high heat until shimmering. Fry the fillets skin-side down for 3 minutes until crisp, then flip and cook for 2 more minutes. Remove to a shallow dish large enough to hold the fish in a single layer.

While the fish is still hot, place the {{onion}}, {{carrot}}, {{scotch-bonnet}}, {{pepper-red}}, {{allspice}}, {{thyme}}, {{white-wine-vinegar}}, and {{caster-sugar}} in a small saucepan. Bring to a boil, stir once, and immediately pour the hot pickle over the fish. The vegetables should still have some bite — they are not cooked through. Leave to cool for at least 30 minutes before serving. Serve at room temperature.

items.cause: Pan not hot enough or fish too wet before flouring
items.fix: Pat fish completely dry before seasoning. The oil should be very hot before the fish goes in — it should sizzle immediately on contact

items.cause: Too much vinegar or no sugar to balance
items.fix: Add another teaspoon of sugar to the pickle. The vinegar flavour softens as the dish sits

items.cause: Pickle cooked for too long before pouring
items.fix: The vegetables only need to come to the boil — pour immediately. They continue to soften from the residual heat of the fish

content.text: Escovitch is the definitive Jamaican Friday night and Saturday morning dish — the fish that comes out at beach cookouts, served from trays at roadside stalls, eaten at room temperature with festival and hard-dough bread. It is not a hot dish; the cooling and resting are part of the technique. In the Jamaican diaspora in Britain it appears as a party and Sunday food, a dish that connects to the coastal eating culture of Jamaica where fresh fish is pickled against the heat.
